runs.interpretation: Secondary-earner substitution-response elasticity used in a U.S. microsimulation model, representing the response coefficient multiplying the bounded relative change in the effective marginal wage for secondary earners.
citations: Eissa & Liebman (1996) - labor supply responses of single mothers to the EITC, compensated elasticity estimates around 0.5–1.0
CBO (2012) - labor supply modeling uses secondary-earner elasticities in the range of 0.5–1.0 for hours and participation
Blau & Kahn (2007) - female labor supply elasticities in the U.S. typically 0.3–0.7 for substitution effects
runs.reasoningSummary: Secondary earners (typically married women or second earners in a household) have well-documented higher labor supply elasticities than primary earners. The empirical literature on compensated (substitution) elasticities for secondary earners in the U.S. clusters roughly between 0.3 and 1.0, with many microsimulation models (e.g., CBO's labor supply model, Tax Policy Center simulations) using values around 0.5 as a central assumption. The lower bound reflects more recent estimates using better identification, while the upper tail reflects older studies or participation-margin-focused estimates. I center my belief at 0.5, with meaningful uncertainty spanning 0.2 to 1.0 at the 5th–95th percentile range.

runs.interpretation: Secondary-earner substitution-response elasticity used in a U.S. microsimulation model, representing the response coefficient multiplying the bounded relative change in the effective marginal wage for secondary earners.
citations: Eissa & Liebman (1996), QJE – estimated large labor supply elasticities for secondary earners (married women) in response to EITC expansions
CBO (2012) labor supply model documentation – uses compensated wage elasticities around 0.25–0.5 for secondary earners in tax simulations
Blau & Kahn (2007), JEL – survey of female labor supply elasticities showing Marshallian elasticities typically 0.3–0.8 for married women
runs.reasoningSummary: Secondary earners (typically married women) in the U.S. have consistently shown higher labor supply elasticities than primary earners in the empirical literature. Standard microsimulation models (e.g., CBO, Tax Policy Center) commonly use compensated (substitution) elasticities in the range of 0.3–0.7 for this group. The literature on female labor supply (Blau & Kahn, Eissa & Liebman) generally finds Hicksian substitution elasticities around 0.5, sometimes up to 1.0. A central value of 0.5 reflects the modal assumption used in U.S. tax-benefit microsimulations, with uncertainty spanning roughly 0.2 to 1.0 across plausible modeling choices.

runs.reasoningSummary: The labor economics literature consistently finds that secondary earners (typically married women) have higher labor supply elasticities than primary earners. Compensated (substitution) elasticities for secondary earners in the U.S. are commonly estimated in the range of 0.3–0.8, with many microsimulation models (e.g., Tax Policy Center, CBO) using values around 0.5 for the substitution elasticity. Earlier studies found higher values (~0.7–1.0), while more recent work finds lower values (~0.2–0.4), reflecting secular changes in female labor force participation. A median of 0.5 reflects a central tendency across the literature and common simulation practice, with uncertainty spanning roughly 0.2 to 1.0.

runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on secondary (typically married women) earner labor supply consistently finds higher elasticities than primary earners. Extensive-margin elasticities for secondary earners typically range from 0.3 to 1.0 in U.S. studies, with a central tendency around 0.5. U.S. microsimulation models (e.g., Tax Policy Center, CBO) commonly adopt substitution elasticities in the 0.3–0.7 range for secondary earners, sometimes up to 1.0 for the extensive margin. A value of 0.5 is a reasonable central estimate reflecting the compensated wage elasticity used in these simulation contexts.

runs.reasoningSummary: Secondary earners (typically married women) have long been documented to have higher labor supply elasticities than primary earners. The empirical literature from the 1980s–2000s found compensated wage elasticities for married women in the range of 0.5–1.0, though more recent estimates (post-2000) tend to be lower, around 0.3–0.6, reflecting declining sensitivity as female labor force participation has risen. U.S. microsimulation models (e.g., those used by CBO or Tax Policy Center) commonly calibrate secondary-earner substitution elasticities around 0.5, sometimes using values between 0.3 and 0.7. The upper tail reflects older, higher estimates and uncertainty about the exact parameterization used in any specific model.

runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on secondary-earner (typically married women) labor supply elasticities in the U.S. consistently finds compensated wage elasticities in the range of 0.3 to 0.7. The bounded-relative-change framing (as used in tools like PSL-Foundation's Tax-Brain or similar) typically uses a value near 0.5. Uncertainty spans roughly 0.2 to 1.0 at the tails, given variation across datasets, time periods, and model specifications.
